{"additions":151,"body":"## Summary
- Add committed `.claude/settings.json` with project-wide allow list: `WebSearch`, `WebFetch`, Context7 MCP tools, read-only file tools, and common bash commands (git/inspect, package managers, `gh` read+write).
- Vendor the `start-wt` skill (`SKILL.md` + `create-worktree.sh`) into `.claude/skills/` so teammates get it on clone without installing it user-side.
- Gitignore `.claude/settings.local.json` so per-user overrides stay local.

## Test plan
- [ ] Pull the branch in a fresh checkout and confirm `start-wt` appears in the skills list.
- [ ] Run a tool covered by the allow list (e.g. `gh pr list`) and confirm no permission prompt.
- [ ] Confirm `.claude/settings.local.json` (if present locally) does not show up in `git status`.

## Heads-up for reviewers
Once merged, future `start-wt` invocations will try to symlink `.claude/` into a new worktree where a real `.claude/` directory now exists. The `ln -s` line in `create-worktree.sh` will fail. Worth a follow-up to make that script idempotent (skip when the target already exists, or symlink subpaths instead of the whole dir).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)","changedFiles":4,"deletions":0,"headRefName":"chore/allow-start-wt-skill","state":"OPEN","title":"Track shared .claude/ config + start-wt skill"}

- Vendor the `start-wt` skill (`SKILL.md` + `create-worktree.sh`) into `.claude/skills/` so teammates get it on clone without installing it user-side.
- Gitignore `.claude/settings.local.json` so per-user overrides stay local.

## Test plan
- [ ] Pull the branch in a fresh checkout and confirm `start-wt` appears in the skills list.
- [ ] Run a tool covered by the allow list (e.g. `gh pr list`) and confirm no permission prompt.
- [ ] Confirm `.claude/settings.local.json` (if present locally) does not show up in `git status`.

## Heads-up for reviewers
Once merged, future `start-wt` invocations will try to symlink `.claude/` into a new worktree where a real `.claude/` directory now exists. The `ln -s` line in `create-worktree.sh` will fail. Worth a follow-up to make that script idempotent (skip when the target already exists, or symlink subpaths instead of the whole dir).

@@ -0,0 +1,48 @@

+      "Bash(git branch:*)",
+      "Bash(git rev-parse:*)",

+      "Bash(ls:*)",
+      "Bash(cat:*)",
+      "Bash(pwd)",
+      "Bash(find:*)",
+      "Bash(grep:*)",
+      "Bash(rg:*)",
+      "Bash(npm test:*)",
+      "Bash(npm run:*)",
+      "Bash(npm install:*)",
+      "Bash(npm ci:*)",
+      "Bash(pnpm:*)",
+      "Bash(yarn:*)",
+      "Bash(gh pr view:*)",
+      "Bash(gh pr list:*)",
+      "Bash(gh pr checks:*)",
+      "Bash(gh pr diff:*)",
+      "Bash(gh issue view:*)",
+      "Bash(gh issue list:*)",
+      "Bash(gh run view:*)",
+      "Bash(gh run list:*)",

+      "Bash(gh pr create:*)",
+      "Bash(gh pr comment:*)",
+      "Bash(gh pr merge:*)",
+      "Bash(gh pr edit:*)",
+      "Bash(gh pr review:*)"
+    ]
+  }
+}
diff --git a/.claude/skills/start-wt/SKILL.md b/.claude/skills/start-wt/SKILL.md
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..06a7b4b
--- /dev/null
+++ b/.claude/skills/start-wt/SKILL.md
@@ -0,0 +1,61 @@
+---
+name: start-wt
+description: Start a new feature using a git worktree. Takes a plain-language description of what you plan to build, proposes a branch name for your approval, creates the worktree as a sibling to the project root (same pattern as the wt() shell function), symlinks tool configs (.claude, .cursor, etc.) and copies .env, then offers to clear context for a fresh start in the new worktree.
+---
+
+You are setting up a new feature branch using a git worktree. The user has described what they want to build in `$ARGUMENTS`.
+
+## Step 1 — Parse the feature description
+
+Read `$ARGUMENTS`. If empty or vague, ask "What are you planning to build?" before proceeding.
+
+## Step 2 — Propose a branch name
+
+From the description, generate **one branch name**:
+- Format: `kebab-case`, 2–4 words
+- Prefer type prefixes when obvious: `feat/`, `fix/`, `chore/`
+- Good: `feat/user-auth`, `fix/invoice-pdf`
+- Avoid: generic words (`new-stuff`, `wip`), dates, underscores
+
+Show the candidate via `AskUserQuestion`. **Do not proceed until confirmed.**
+
+## Step 3 — Pre-flight checks
+
+```bash
+git rev-parse --show-toplevel    # capture as $REPO_PATH
+git worktree list                 # check if target path already exists
+git status                        # warn (don't block) on uncommitted changes
+```
+
+Target path: `$(dirname $REPO_PATH)/$(basename $REPO_PATH)-worktrees/$BRANCH`. If it already appears in `git worktree list`, stop and tell the user to `git worktree remove <path>` first.
+
+## Step 4 — Create the worktree
+
+```bash
+"${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR}/.claude/skills/start-wt/create-worktree.sh" "$BRANCH"
+```
+
+The script handles worktree creation, symlinks config dirs (`.claude`, `.cursor`, `.instrumental`, `.agent_os`), and copies env files. It outputs `WORKTREE_PATH=...` on stdout.
+
+If it fails, show the error and stop.
+
+## Step 5 — Enter the worktree
+
+Use the `EnterWorktree` tool with `path: "$WORKTREE_PATH"` (from script output). The rest of the conversation operates inside the worktree.
+
+If `EnterWorktree` fails, fall back to printing `cd "$WORKTREE_PATH" && claude` and stop.
+
+## Step 6 — Summary + clear
+
+Print:
+
+```
+✅  Worktree ready and entered
+
+   Branch : <branch>
+   Path   : <worktree-path>
+
+   Run /clear next so we start the feature with a fresh context.
+```
+
+End your turn — only the user can `/clear`.
diff --git a/.claude/skills/start-wt/create-worktree.sh b/.claude/skills/start-wt/create-worktree.sh
new file mode 100755
index 0000000..ba295bf
--- /dev/null
+++ b/.claude/skills/start-wt/create-worktree.sh
@@ -0,0 +1,39 @@
+#!/usr/bin/env bash
+set -euo pipefail
+
+BRANCH="${1:?Usage: create-worktree.sh <branch-name>}"
+REPO_PATH="${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR:-$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)}"
+PROJECT_NAME="$(basename "$REPO_PATH")"
+WORKTREE_PARENT="$(dirname "$REPO_PATH")/${PROJECT_NAME}-worktrees"
+WORKTREE_PATH="${WORKTREE_PARENT}/${BRANCH//\//-}"
+
+# Progress to /dev/tty — stdout reserved for Claude
+log() { echo "$*" > /dev/tty 2>/dev/null || true; }
+
+log "Creating worktree (branch: $BRANCH)..."
+
+mkdir -p "$WORKTREE_PARENT"
+git worktree add -b "$BRANCH" "$WORKTREE_PATH" >/dev/null 2>&1
+
+# Symlink shared config dirs — edits stay in sync across worktrees
+log "  Symlinking config dirs..."
+for d in .claude .cursor .instrumental .agent_os; do
+  if [ -d "${REPO_PATH}/$d" ]; then
+    ln -s "${REPO_PATH}/$d" "${WORKTREE_PATH}/$d"
+    log "    linked $d"
+  fi
+done
+
+# Copy env files — these often need per-worktree tweaks
+log "  Copying env files..."
+for f in .env .env.local; do
+  if [ -f "${REPO_PATH}/$f" ]; then
+    cp "${REPO_PATH}/$f" "${WORKTREE_PATH}/$f"
+    log "    copied $f"
+  fi
+done
+
+log "Worktree ready."
+
+# The only thing on stdout — Claude parses this
+echo "WORKTREE_PATH=$WORKTREE_PATH"
diff --git a/.gitignore b/.gitignore
index cda1d39..74eeb60 100644
--- a/.gitignore
+++ b/.gitignore
@@ -11,3 +11,6 @@ bin/
 
 # Generated by tools/viewer.sh — local index of agent-log files.
 tools/log-index.json
+
+# Per-user Claude Code overrides — keep out of the shared repo.
+.claude/settings.local.json

content.thinking:
Let me analyze the PR carefully:

1. **`.claude/settings.json`** - Adds a permissions allow list
2. **`.claude/skills/start-wt/SKILL.md`** - New skill for creating git worktrees
3. **`.claude/skills/start-wt/create-worktree.sh`** - Shell script for worktree creation
4. **`.gitignore`** - Adds `.claude/settings.local.json` to gitignore

Let me look at the diff carefully for correctness bugs, security issues, performance issues, and code quality concerns.
Looking at the shell script, I'm checking for command injection vulnerabilities. The branch name is properly quoted when passed to git commands, and the path construction safely replaces forward slashes with hyphens before using the variable in quoted contexts, so the command injection risks appear minimal.

However, there's a significant issue with error handling: the script redirects stderr to `/dev/null` when running `git worktree add`, which means if the command fails (like when a branch already exists), the user won't see any error message explaining what went wrong. The script will still exit due to the `set -euo pipefail` setting, but silently, which violates the requirement to show errors when operations fail.

I'm also noticing the script creates symlinks from the worktree back to the original repository, which could potentially introduce symlink-related security concerns depending on how the worktree directory is used. The `ln -s` error will actually be visible to the user since it goes to stderr, but the worktree has already been created at that point—so we end up with a partial failure where the worktree exists but the config symlink wasn't set up, and the script exits before printing the worktree path that the skill needs to continue. This is the real breakage the PR acknowledges.

Now I'm looking at the security implications of the allowed bash commands in settings.json, particularly the ones that could have significant consequences like auto-merging PRs, creating PRs, and installing packages from npm, yarn, or pnpm—each of which presents different risk vectors depending on whether the package files or repository state have been tampered with.

The `gh pr merge:*` permission is especially concerning because it's a destructive operation that could execute silently without user confirmation, and when combined with other GitHub CLI permissions like reviewing and editing PRs, it creates a scenario where an agent could autonomously perform substantial git workflow actions. The threat model here really depends on whether this is a trusted project environment, but the breadth of the `gh api:*` permission is worth examining more closely since the GitHub API can accomplish quite a lot.

The `gh api` command opens up access to potentially dangerous operations like creating or modifying webhooks that could exfiltrate code, adding collaborators, changing branch protection rules, or deleting tags and releases. Package manager commands like `npm install`, `yarn`, and `pnpm` without prompting also introduce supply chain risks since malicious postinstall scripts could execute silently if a dependency is compromised. I'm also noticing what looks like a correctness bug in the `create-worktree.sh` script around line 16.

The git worktree command is silencing both stdout and stderr, which means when it fails, the error message is completely hidden from the user even though the script exits due to the `set -e` flag. The `log()` function writes to `/dev/tty`, but since the git command's stderr is redirected to `/dev/null`, the actual error never gets displayed to the skill, violating the requirement to show errors when operations fail.

There's also a known issue where once `.claude/` is tracked in git, the worktree creation will succeed but the symlink step fails with "File exists" since the directory already exists in the new worktree. The PR author is aware of this but merges anyway, which breaks the `start-wt` skill they're also submitting.

Looking at severity, the overly broad `gh api:*` permission is concerning—it creates an SSRF-like risk if Claude can be manipulated into injecting arbitrary API endpoints.  The `npm install:*` / `yarn:*` / `pnpm:*` permissions without prompting pose a supply chain risk. The broken worktree creation is high severity since it directly breaks the primary feature. The `gh pr merge:*` without prompting is medium severity for silent merges, and the silenced stderr is just a diagnostic quality issue.

I should also check whether `.claude/settings.json` is properly ignored in gitignore, since the directory is now being committed.

Looking at the SKILL.md, it references `${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR}` to locate the skill script, and the create-worktree.sh script has a fallback to use `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` if that variable isn't set. But the SKILL.md invocation in step 4 doesn't seem to have a fallback mechanism. The main issues I'm seeing are that the `start-wt` skill will break after this PR merges since `.claude/` becomes git-tracked, which means `git worktree add` will check it out into the new worktree and cause the symlink creation to fail—the PR author knows about this but is merging anyway. Beyond that, the `Bash(gh api:*)` permission is too permissive since it allows any GitHub API call including destructive operations like modifying repo settings or adding collaborators, and `Bash(gh pr merge:*)` similarly allows silent PR merges which is a significant write operation. So the `start-wt` skill will still prompt for permission when it tries to run `git worktree add` since that command isn't in the allow list. That's an inconsistency worth noting.

Now I'm thinking through the `gh api` concern more carefully. With `--method POST` or `-f` flags, this command can make destructive write calls—adding webhooks to repos, deleting branches, modifying repository settings. An attacker or even a confused Claude could cause real damage here.

The permission model should probably distinguish between read and write operations for `gh api` rather than allowing the wildcard. I need to be careful about how confident I am in this assessment though, since `gh api` is just a wrapper around GitHub's REST API.

For the inline comments, I'm identifying specific issues: the symlink creation in `create-worktree.sh` will fail because `.claude/` already exists in the worktree after `git worktree add` runs, which breaks the skill entirely.  The stderr redirection hides error messages that would help with debugging. The `gh api:*` permission is too permissive for write operations, and `gh pr merge:*` allows silent PR merges which is a significant write capability.

The bigger picture issue is that this PR introduces a fundamental conflict: committing `.claude/` means the `start-wt` skill breaks immediately on new worktrees, but the author acknowledges this in their "Heads-up" without actually fixing it. These two changes shouldn't land together without addressing the idempotency problem first.

content.thinking:
Now I have all the information I need. Let me post the inline comments for the issues I found:

1. **`create-worktree.sh` line 21** (the `ln -s` line): Will fail for `.claude/` since it's now tracked by git and will exist in new worktrees - **High** severity. This breaks the skill being vendored in the same PR.

2. **`create-worktree.sh` line 16** (the `git worktree add` line with stderr silenced): **Low** severity - hides error info on failure.

3. **`settings.json` line 41** (`"Bash(gh api:*)"`) - Too broad, allows write API operations - **High** severity.
4. **`settings.json` line 44** (`"Bash(gh pr merge:*)"`) - Allows silent PR merges - **Medium** severity.

The real problem here is architectural: the PR commits `.claude/` as tracked git content and ships the `start-wt` skill in the same change, but the skill immediately breaks because it tries to symlink `.claude/` into worktrees that already have it from the git checkout. The fix is straightforward—just check if the symlink target exists first—but without addressing this, the skill will be broken the moment anyone tries to use it.

content.text: Now I'll post the inline review comments for the issues found.

input.body:
**Self-inflicted breakage: skill will fail on every new worktree after this PR merges.**

**Severity:** High

**Reasoning:** `git worktree add` checks out tracked files, so `.claude/` will already exist as a real directory in the new worktree (it's now committed to the repo). `ln -s` will then fail with "File exists", `set -e` aborts the script, and `WORKTREE_PATH=…` is never printed — the skill can't continue. The fix is a one-liner guard before calling `ln`:

```suggestion
  if [ -d "${REPO_PATH}/$d" ] && [ ! -e "${WORKTREE_PATH}/$d" ]; then
```

This makes the symlink step idempotent (skip when the target already exists). The PR description flags this as a known issue worth a "follow-up", but since both the vendored `.claude/` dir and this script land in the same commit, the skill is broken the moment the PR merges.

content.thinking: Now let me post the remaining inline comments.

input.body:
**stderr silenced on `git worktree add` — failures are diagnosed blind.**

**Severity:** Low

**Reasoning:** `>/dev/null 2>&1` hides the git error message. With `set -e`, a failure (e.g. branch already exists, no disk space, lock conflict) exits the script silently. The `WORKTREE_PATH=…` line is never reached, so the skill just sees a non-zero exit code with no useful message. At minimum, redirect only stdout to suppress the normal git progress output and let stderr through:

```suggestion
git worktree add -b "$BRANCH" "$WORKTREE_PATH" >/dev/null
```

input.body:
**`gh api:*` allows any GitHub API call — including destructive write operations — without prompting.**

**Severity:** High

**Reasoning:** `gh api` with `-X POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE` can modify repository settings, add collaborators, create webhooks pointing at attacker-controlled URLs, delete refs, and more. Allowing this unconditionally means a prompt-injected agent (e.g. from malicious content inside a PR being reviewed) can make arbitrary write API calls without the user ever seeing a confirmation dialog. The pattern is equivalent to whitelisting `curl -X POST https://api.github.com/**`.

Consider splitting it: keep `gh api` for reads but require a prompt for writes, or at minimum scope to specific read-only endpoints (e.g. `Bash(gh api repos/*/pulls:*)`).

content.thinking: Let me post the remaining inline comment for `gh pr merge:*` and then a top-level comment.

input.body:
**`gh pr merge:*` is a destructive write operation that should require confirmation.**

**Severity:** Medium

**Reasoning:** Merging a PR is irreversible and visible to everyone on the repo. Allowing it without a prompt means any agent run in this project can silently merge open PRs — including an agent manipulated by malicious content in a PR description or review comment. The read-only `gh` commands in the list above this line are fine to allow; merge (and similarly `gh pr edit:*`) crosses into write territory that benefits from a human in the loop.

input.command:
gh pr comment 23 --body "The two halves of this PR work against each other: committing \`.claude/\` to git (so worktrees check it out) and shipping a \`start-wt\` skill that immediately tries to \`ln -s .claude/\` into those same worktrees (which already have it). The \`start-wt\` skill will be broken for every new worktree from the moment this merges — see the inline comment on line 21 of \`create-worktree.sh\` for the one-line fix.

The permissions allow-list in \`settings.json\` also has two entries worth narrowing before merge: \`gh api:*\` (any GitHub API write, including webhooks and collaborator changes) and \`gh pr merge:*\` (irreversible, should stay prompt-gated). Details in the inline comments."

content.text:
Four comments posted:

- **High** — `ln -s .claude` in `create-worktree.sh:21` will fail on every new worktree because `.claude/` is now tracked by git and already exists after `git worktree add`. One-line fix included as a suggestion.
- **Low** — `git worktree add … >/dev/null 2>&1` silences stderr, making failures undiagnosable. Should redirect only stdout.
- **High** — `Bash(gh api:*)` allows unconditional GitHub write API calls (webhooks, collaborator changes, ref deletion) — a prompt-injection risk for any agent reviewing PR content.
- **Medium** — `Bash(gh pr merge:*)` is an irreversible write operation that should stay prompt-gated.

result:
Four comments posted:

- **High** — `ln -s .claude` in `create-worktree.sh:21` will fail on every new worktree because `.claude/` is now tracked by git and already exists after `git worktree add`. One-line fix included as a suggestion.
- **Low** — `git worktree add … >/dev/null 2>&1` silences stderr, making failures undiagnosable. Should redirect only stdout.
- **High** — `Bash(gh api:*)` allows unconditional GitHub write API calls (webhooks, collaborator changes, ref deletion) — a prompt-injection risk for any agent reviewing PR content.
- **Medium** — `Bash(gh pr merge:*)` is an irreversible write operation that should stay prompt-gated.
